content.text: For advanced readersIn C++, the result of an expression is one of the following:A value (most commonly)An object or a function. We discuss expressions that return objects in lesson12.2 -- Value categories (lvalues and rvalues).Nothing. These are the result of non-value returning function calls (covered in lesson2.3 -- Void functions (non-value returning functions)) that are called only for their side effectsFor now, to keep things simple, we’ll assume expressions are evaluated to produce values.

content.text: For now, to keep things simple, we’ll assume expressions are evaluated to produce values.

content.code:
2               // 2 is a literal that evaluates to value 2
"Hello world!"  // "Hello world!" is a literal that evaluates to text "Hello world!"
x               // x is a variable that evaluates to the value held by variable x
2 + 3           // operator+ uses operands 2 and 3 to evaluate to value 5
five()          // evaluates to the return value of function five()

content.text:
For advanced readersExpressions involving operators with side effects are a little more tricky:x = 5           // x = 5 has side effect of assigning 5 to x, evaluates to x
x = 2 + 3       // has side effect of assigning 5 to x, evaluates to x
std::cout << x  // has side effect of printing value of x to console, evaluates to std::cout

Expressions involving operators with side effects are a little more tricky:x = 5           // x = 5 has side effect of assigning 5 to x, evaluates to x
x = 2 + 3       // has side effect of assigning 5 to x, evaluates to x
std::cout << x  // has side effect of printing value of x to console, evaluates to std::cout

content.code:
x = 5           // x = 5 has side effect of assigning 5 to x, evaluates to x
x = 2 + 3       // has side effect of assigning 5 to x, evaluates to x
std::cout << x  // has side effect of printing value of x to console, evaluates to std::cout

content.text: Key insightWherever a single value is expected in C++, you can use an value-producing expression instead, and the expression will be evaluated to produce a single value.
Wherever a single value is expected in C++, you can use an value-producing expression instead, and the expression will be evaluated to produce a single value.

content.code: int x{ 2 + 3 }; // 2 + 3 is an expression that has no semicolon -- the semicolon is at the end of the statement containing the expression

content.text: Related contentWe cover side effects in lesson1.9 -- Introduction to literals and operators).
We cover side effects in lesson1.9 -- Introduction to literals and operators).

content.code:
2               // 2 is a literal that evaluates to value 2
2 + 3           // 2 + 3 uses operator+ to evaluate to value 5
x = 4 + 5       // 4 + 5 evaluates to value 9, which is then assigned to variable x

content.text: Question #1
Statements are used when we want the program to perform an action. Expressions are used when we want the program to calculate a value.
Statement does not contain an expression. This is a variable definition statement.xis not an expression in this context because it is being defined, not evaluated.
Statement contains an expression.int xis a variable definition. The=is part of the syntax for copy initialization. The initializer to the right of the equals sign is an expression.
Expression statement.x = 5is a call tooperator=with two operands:xand5. The semicolon makes it an expression statement.
Function calls are part of an expression, so this is an expression statement.

content.code: std::cout << x; // Hint: operator<< is a binary operator.

content.text: operator<<is a binary operator, sostd::coutmust be the left-hand operand, andxmust be the right-hand operand. Since that’s the entire statement, this must be an expression statement.
